reasoning:
To resolve YES, the NBER Business Cycle Dating Committee must declare that a recession began (the peak of the business cycle occurred) at some point in 2023 or 2024. The criteria for an NBER recession involve a significant decline in economic activity spread across the economy, lasting more than a few months, normally visible in real GDP, real income, employment, industrial production, and wholesale-retail sales. 

Economic data from late 2024 definitively rules this out. Real GDP grew at a solid annualized rate of 2.8% to 3.1% in Q3 and 2.3% in Q4 (search_1). Nonfarm payrolls surged by 256,000 in December 2024, and the unemployment rate ticked down to 4.1% (search_3). Instead of peaking and turning downward, the economy was robust and accelerating or maintaining steady growth across all the primary indicators the NBER evaluates. Furthermore, heading into 2025, indicators remain strongly positive: the January 2025 jobs report showed another 143,000 jobs added, and Q1 2025 GDPNow projections are nearly 3% (search_2). There is essentially no legitimate data-driven basis for the NBER to retroactively determine that the economy entered a recession in 2024. Therefore, I place the probability at the allowable floor of 2%.
